summary: Fowler breaks his rule against book reviews to recommend 'The Thrilling Adventures of Lovelace and Babbage' by Sydney Padua. The graphic novel combines entertaining steampunk alternate history with genuine Victorian-era computing history. After depicting the real collaboration between Ada Lovelace and Charles Babbage, the book enters a pocket universe where Lovelace survives and they complete the Analytical Engine. Fowler particularly praises the extensive footnotes that connect the fictional adventures to real history, and notes this is a book that works best in physical form.
key_points: The book effectively combines graphic novel storytelling with substantive historical information about early computing pioneers
It uses an alternate universe premise where Lovelace lives and the Analytical Engine gets built, enabling creative fictional adventures
The footnotes (with their own endnotes) provide the real historical context that makes the fictional elements meaningful
Fowler considers it ideal for his technical readership as an enjoyable diversion
The physical book format is essential to the reading experience
money_quotes: As a rule, I don't do book reviews. My main area of activity is software development, and I know too many authors.
It opens with a comic book narrative of the collaboration between Countess Lovelace and Charles Babbage, which explains why we refer to Countess Lovelace as the first computer programmer.
The book is imaginative, funny, and also very informative about the real Victorian universe.
All in all it's a great book for dipping in and out of, and I think most people who read my serious stuff will find it a most excellent diversion.
It's also a book that screams to only be a physical book.
key_insight: A graphic novel about computing pioneers can be both genuinely entertaining and historically informative, making it an ideal diversion for technically-minded readers.
